title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a meteorologist called Ethan Reed talking about his career as a professional storm chaser in the United States. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a meteorologist called Ethan Reed talking about his career as a professional storm chaser in the United States. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ethan Reed discusses the scientific goals and safety challenges of tracking supercell thunderstorms.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi everyone, I’m Ethan. To most people, the idea of driving towards a massive tornado seems like a nightmare, but for me, it’s a typical Tuesday in May. I’m a professional storm chaser. My fascination with extreme weather didn't start with a big Hollywood movie; it actually began when I witnessed a [7]heavy hailstorm[/7] while visiting my grandparents in Kansas as a boy. I was amazed that the sky could produce ice stones the size of baseballs in the middle of summer. That curiosity led me to study atmospheric science at university.

Storm chasing is often depicted as a high-speed thrill ride, but the reality involves a lot of waiting in petrol stations. We spend about 90% of our time analyzing data on laptops. The goal isn't just to see a tornado, but to deploy sensors. The most difficult part of the setup is the [8]deployment timing[/8] of our equipment. You have to place the sensors directly in the path of the storm and then get out of the way before the wind speeds become life-threatening. If you’re thirty seconds too late, you lose the equipment and potentially your life.

Our vehicle is essentially a mobile laboratory. It’s been reinforced with steel plates to protect us from flying debris. However, even with all that protection, we still face unexpected issues. I’ve found that [9]shattered windows[/9] are the most frequent form of damage we have to deal with, usually caused by small stones being kicked up from the road rather than the storm itself. It’s a constant maintenance battle to keep the truck roadworthy during the peak season.

One of the most valuable tools we use is a portable Doppler radar. It allows us to see the internal structure of a thunderstorm in real-time. I noticed that the [10]color intensity[/10] on the radar screen is the best indicator of where a tornado might be forming. We look for a specific 'hook' shape that suggests the air is rotating. It’s a bit like being a detective, looking for clues in a chaotic environment.

People often ask about the emotional side of the job. It’s hard to stay objective when you see the destruction a storm can cause. I believe that having a [11]calm temperament[/11] is the most vital trait for anyone in this field. When the sirens are going off and the sky turns an eerie shade of green, you have to be able to make rational decisions under extreme pressure. If you panic, you make mistakes, and in this job, mistakes are final.

Beyond the science, I also work as a freelance photographer. I’ve found that my [12]lightning photos[/12] are what people are most interested in buying. There’s something about the raw energy of a bolt hitting the ground that captures the public's imagination. It’s a way for me to fund the more expensive scientific parts of our expeditions.

Looking back, the biggest change in the industry has been the number of people on the road. 'Chaser convergence' is a real problem now. I’m worried that the [13]traffic congestion[/13] on narrow country roads is making it impossible for emergency services to reach affected towns. It’s a dangerous trend that we’re trying to address through better industry regulation.

Next year, I’m planning to lead a specialized [14]safety seminar[/14] for amateur chasers. I want to teach them how to read the clouds and respect the power of nature without putting themselves or others at risk. It’s a beautiful, violent world out there, and I want to make sure everyone gets home safely. Thanks for listening.

dialogue.speaker: ethan
